---
title: "AI Salary Disparities"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/Chart.js/4.4.1/chart.umd.js"></script>
---

```{=html}
<!-- ════════════════════════════ HERO ════════════════════════════ -->
<div class="hero">
  <div class="hero-inner">
    <div class="hero-eyebrow">● Lightcast US · 29,798 postings · May–Sep 2024</div>
    <h1>Salary disparities across<br>disciplines — who benefits<br>most from <span>AI's rise?</span></h1>
    <p class="hero-sub">
      A data-driven look at how the AI boom is reshaping pay across skills, experience levels, and geographies.
      Three interactive views, drawn from nearly 30,000 US job postings, reveal where the AI premium is real,
      where it's hype, and where it inverts entirely.
    </p>
    <div class="hero-meta">
      <div class="hero-meta-item">
        <div class="hero-meta-label">Source</div>
        <div class="hero-meta-value">Lightcast US</div>
      </div>
      <div class="hero-meta-item">
        <div class="hero-meta-label">Postings</div>
        <div class="hero-meta-value">29,798</div>
      </div>
      <div class="hero-meta-item">
        <div class="hero-meta-label">Period</div>
        <div class="hero-meta-value">May – Sep 2024</div>
      </div>
      <div class="hero-meta-item">
        <div class="hero-meta-label">Authors</div>
        <div class="hero-meta-value">Hasnat & Bakdaulet</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ═══════════════════════ AUTHORS STRIP ═══════════════════════ -->
<div class="authors-strip">
  <div class="authors-strip-inner">
    <div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:1rem; flex-wrap:wrap;">
      <span class="authors-label">Authored by</span>
      <div class="authors-names">
        <span class="author-chip"><span class="author-dot"></span>Hasnat</span>
        <span class="author-chip"><span class="author-dot"></span>Bakdaulet</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="authors-meta">Boston University · MS Applied Business Analytics</div>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- ═══════════════════════ SECTION INTRO ═══════════════════════ -->
<div class="section" style="padding-bottom:2rem;">
  <div class="section-eyebrow">▸ Three views, one question</div>
  <h2 class="section-title">Where AI actually pays — and where it doesn't</h2>
  <p class="section-lead">
    The "AI premium" is real, but it's not evenly distributed. Some skills, career stages, and states see
    double-digit pay bumps; others see the opposite. Each chart below is interactive — hover for exact figures.
  </p>
</div>

<!-- ════════════════════════ VISUAL 1 ════════════════════════ -->
<div class="section" style="padding-top:0;">

  <div class="viz-card">
    <div class="viz-header">
      <div class="viz-number">01</div>
      <div>
        <div class="viz-eyebrow">Skill-level analysis</div>
        <h3 class="viz-headline">Generative AI commands a 49% premium — Excel pays 26% less</h3>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-explanation">
      <p>
        At the top of the salary ladder sit the skills that <strong>build</strong> AI systems — Generative AI (+49%),
        Kubernetes (+34%), Docker (+29%), and AWS (+27%). At the bottom sit the tools that <strong>consume</strong>
        their outputs — Excel (−26%), Power BI (−17%), and Tableau (−16%) all post salaries well below market median.
      </p>
      <p>
        The takeaway: <strong>employers are paying steep premiums for AI infrastructure and ML engineering skills</strong>,
        while traditional analyst tooling has become commoditized. This is association, not causation — but the gap
        is striking and consistent across thousands of postings.
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-body">
      <div style="font-size:13px; color:var(--slate); margin-bottom:4px;">Median salary of postings mentioning each skill, vs. postings that don't</div>
      <div style="font-size:12px; color:var(--slate); margin-bottom:12px;">Lightcast US job postings, May–Sep 2024 · n = 29,798 full-time postings with salary</div>
      <div style="display:flex; gap:16px; margin-bottom:8px; font-size:12px; color:var(--navy-3);">
        <span style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:6px;"><span style="width:10px; height:10px; border-radius:2px; background:#1D9E75;"></span>Pays above market</span>
        <span style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:6px;"><span style="width:10px; height:10px; border-radius:2px; background:#D85A30;"></span>Pays below market</span>
      </div>
      <div style="position:relative; width:100%; height:760px;">
        <canvas id="skillPremium" role="img" aria-label="Horizontal bar chart of salary premium by skill."></canvas>
      </div>
      <div style="font-size:11px; color:var(--slate); margin-top:8px; line-height:1.5;">
        Read as association, not causation: the skill mix on a posting reflects the kind of role being hired for.
        A "−26% Excel" posting is typically a different job from a "+49% Generative AI" posting, not the same job paid less.
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- ════════════════════════ VISUAL 2 ════════════════════════ -->
  <div class="viz-card">
    <div class="viz-header">
      <div class="viz-number">02</div>
      <div>
        <div class="viz-eyebrow">Experience-level analysis</div>
        <h3 class="viz-headline">The AI premium is largest for entry-level — and disappears at the top</h3>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-explanation">
      <p>
        For entry-level workers (0–2 years), AI postings pay <strong>19.5% more</strong> than non-AI peers — a $15K bump
        on a median ~$80K base. The gap shrinks to roughly 10% for mid- and senior-level roles, then collapses to essentially
        zero at lead/principal (9+ years).
      </p>
      <p>
        The pattern suggests AI is currently a <strong>ladder for early-career workers</strong>, not a multiplier at
        the top. Once you're senior enough, leadership and domain expertise dominate compensation, and the AI label
        no longer adds a separate premium.
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-body">
      <div style="font-size:12px; color:var(--slate); margin-bottom:14px;">
        Median posted salary by minimum-years-experience bucket · Lightcast US, May–Sep 2024 · n = 23,116 postings with experience specified
      </div>
      <div style="position:relative; width:100%; height:380px;">
        <canvas id="expChart" role="img" aria-label="Grouped bar chart of median salary by experience level."></canvas>
      </div>
      <div style="font-size:11px; color:var(--slate); margin-top:10px; line-height:1.5;">
        AI postings command a $15K bump for entry-level roles but the gap closes by senior level and inverts slightly
        at lead/principal — at the top of the ladder, seniority dominates and AI no longer pays a premium.
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- ════════════════════════ VISUAL 3 ════════════════════════ -->
  <div class="viz-card">
    <div class="viz-header">
      <div class="viz-number">03</div>
      <div>
        <div class="viz-eyebrow">Geographic analysis</div>
        <h3 class="viz-headline">Where the AI premium is biggest — not always where you'd guess</h3>
      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-explanation">
      <p>
        Among major tech states, <strong>Washington (+33%)</strong> and <strong>California (+20%)</strong> show the
        steepest AI premiums — consistent with cloud and AI hyperscalers concentrated there. Smaller-volume states
        like Rhode Island and South Carolina top the chart, but with thin samples (~30 AI postings each), treat as directional.
      </p>
      <p>
        <strong>Kentucky inverts the pattern</strong> with a −17% premium: its AI postings skew toward lower-paying
        analyst work rather than ML engineering, a reminder that "AI jobs" isn't a monolithic category — composition
        matters as much as label.
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="viz-body">
      <div style="font-size:12px; color:var(--slate); margin-bottom:14px;">
        AI vs non-AI median salary gap, states with ≥200 postings · Lightcast US, May–Sep 2024
      </div>
      <div style="position:relative; width:100%; height:460px;">
        <canvas id="stateChart" role="img" aria-label="Horizontal bar chart of AI premium by state."></canvas>
      </div>
      <div style="font-size:11px; color:var(--slate); margin-top:10px; line-height:1.5;">
        Among major tech states, Washington (+33%) and California (+20%) show the steepest premiums — consistent with
        cloud and AI hyperscalers concentrated there. Smaller-volume states like Rhode Island and South Carolina top
        the chart but the AI sample sizes are small (~30–35 postings), so treat as directional. Kentucky inverts:
        its AI postings skew toward lower-paying analyst work.
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- ═══════════════════════ KEY INSIGHTS ═══════════════════════ -->
  <div class="section-eyebrow" style="margin-top:1rem;">▸ At a glance</div>
  <h2 class="section-title">The headline numbers</h2>

  <div class="insight-row">
    <div class="insight-card">
      <div class="insight-stat">+49%</div>
      <div class="insight-label">Generative AI premium — the largest pay gap of any skill in the dataset</div>
    </div>
    <div class="insight-card">
      <div class="insight-stat">+19.5%</div>
      <div class="insight-label">AI premium for entry-level workers — drops to near-zero at lead/principal</div>
    </div>
    <div class="insight-card">
      <div class="insight-stat">−26%</div>
      <div class="insight-label">Excel salary discount — traditional analyst tools pay below market</div>
    </div>
  </div>

</div>

<!-- ════════════════════════ CHART SCRIPTS ════════════════════════ -->
<script>
// Wait for Chart.js to load
function initCharts() {
  if (typeof Chart === 'undefined') {
    setTimeout(initCharts, 100);
    return;
  }

  // ─────────── CHART 1: Skill Premium ───────────
  const skillData = [
    {s: 'Generative AI', v: 49.1, n: 84},
    {s: 'Kubernetes', v: 34.2, n: 387},
    {s: 'Docker', v: 28.7, n: 328},
    {s: 'AWS', v: 27.3, n: 1953},
    {s: 'Spark', v: 23.8, n: 535},
    {s: 'Azure', v: 19.9, n: 2213},
    {s: 'TensorFlow / PyTorch', v: 17.8, n: 103},
    {s: 'Computer Vision', v: 17.8, n: 37},
    {s: 'Machine Learning', v: 17.0, n: 1984},
    {s: 'MLOps', v: 16.2, n: 119},
    {s: 'NLP', v: 14.0, n: 140},
    {s: 'Hadoop', v: 5.7, n: 634},
    {s: 'Python', v: -1.8, n: 5312},
    {s: 'SQL', v: -13.2, n: 10242},
    {s: 'R', v: -13.5, n: 2647},
    {s: 'Tableau', v: -15.9, n: 5720},
    {s: 'Power BI', v: -17.4, n: 4669},
    {s: 'Excel', v: -26.4, n: 6194},
  ];
  new Chart(document.getElementById('skillPremium'), {
    type: 'bar',
    data: {
      labels: skillData.map(d => d.s),
      datasets: [{
        data: skillData.map(d => d.v),
        backgroundColor: skillData.map(d => d.v >= 0 ? '#1D9E75' : '#D85A30'),
        borderWidth: 0,
        borderRadius: 3,
        barPercentage: 0.78,
      }]
    },
    options: {
      indexAxis: 'y',
      responsive: true,
      maintainAspectRatio: false,
      layout: { padding: { right: 28 } },
      plugins: {
        legend: { display: false },
        tooltip: {
          callbacks: {
            label: (ctx) => {
              const d = skillData[ctx.dataIndex];
              return `${d.v > 0 ? '+' : ''}${d.v}% premium · ${d.n.toLocaleString()} postings`;
            }
          }
        }
      },
      scales: {
        x: {
          grid: { color: 'rgba(128,128,128,0.12)' },
          ticks: { color: '#64748b', callback: (v) => (v > 0 ? '+' : '') + v + '%' },
          title: { display: true, text: 'Salary premium vs. postings without this skill', color: '#64748b', font: { size: 12 } }
        },
        y: {
          grid: { display: false },
          ticks: { color: '#334155', font: { size: 12 } }
        }
      },
      animation: { duration: 700 }
    }
  });

  // ─────────── CHART 2: Experience Level ───────────
  const expLabels = ['Entry (0–2 yrs)', 'Mid (3–5 yrs)', 'Senior (6–8 yrs)', 'Lead/Principal (9+ yrs)'];
  const aiData    = [96100, 121650, 147650, 148700];
  const nonAiData = [80400, 110000, 133650, 149695];
  const premiums  = ['+19.5%', '+10.6%', '+10.5%', '−0.7%'];

  new Chart(document.getElementById('expChart'), {
    type: 'bar',
    data: {
      labels: expLabels,
      datasets: [
        { label: 'AI postings',    data: aiData,    backgroundColor: '#1D9E75', borderRadius: 3, borderWidth: 0 },
        { label: 'Non-AI postings', data: nonAiData, backgroundColor: '#A8B5C4', borderRadius: 3, borderWidth: 0 },
      ]
    },
    options: {
      responsive: true,
      maintainAspectRatio: false,
      plugins: {
        legend: { position: 'top', labels: { color: '#334155', font: { size: 12 } } },
        tooltip: { callbacks: { label: (ctx) => `${ctx.dataset.label}: $${ctx.raw.toLocaleString()}` } },
      },
      scales: {
        y: {
          beginAtZero: true,
          grid: { color: 'rgba(128,128,128,0.12)' },
          ticks: { color: '#64748b', callback: (v) => '$' + (v/1000) + 'K' },
          title: { display: true, text: 'Median posted salary', color: '#64748b' }
        },
        x: { grid: { display: false }, ticks: { color: '#334155', font: { size: 12 } } }
      },
      animation: { duration: 700 },
    },
    plugins: [{
      id: 'premiumLabels',
      afterDatasetsDraw(chart) {
        const { ctx, scales: { x, y } } = chart;
        ctx.save();
        ctx.font = '600 12px sans-serif';
        ctx.textAlign = 'center';
        expLabels.forEach((_, i) => {
          const top = Math.min(y.getPixelForValue(aiData[i]), y.getPixelForValue(nonAiData[i]));
          const xpos = x.getPixelForValue(i);
          ctx.fillStyle = premiums[i].startsWith('−') ? '#D85A30' : '#1D9E75';
          ctx.fillText(premiums[i], xpos, top - 8);
        });
        ctx.restore();
      }
    }]
  });

  // ─────────── CHART 3: State Premium ───────────
  const stateData = [
    {s: 'Rhode Island', v: 52.2, n: 244, hl: false},
    {s: 'South Carolina', v: 33.2, n: 239, hl: false},
    {s: 'Washington', v: 32.6, n: 945, hl: true},
    {s: 'Oregon', v: 24.0, n: 560, hl: false},
    {s: 'Nevada', v: 23.2, n: 270, hl: false},
    {s: 'California', v: 20.3, n: 3868, hl: true},
    {s: 'Kansas', v: 20.0, n: 303, hl: false},
    {s: 'Missouri', v: 19.0, n: 545, hl: false},
    {s: 'Utah', v: 19.0, n: 274, hl: false},
    {s: 'Maryland', v: 18.3, n: 552, hl: false},
    {s: 'New York', v: 17.8, n: 1946, hl: true},
    {s: 'Massachusetts', v: 14.5, n: 737, hl: true},
    {s: 'Texas', v: 9.6, n: 2537, hl: true},
    {s: 'Kentucky', v: -17.4, n: 288, hl: false},
  ];
  new Chart(document.getElementById('stateChart'), {
    type: 'bar',
    data: {
      labels: stateData.map(d => d.s),
      datasets: [{
        data: stateData.map(d => d.v),
        backgroundColor: stateData.map(d => d.v < 0 ? '#D85A30' : (d.hl ? '#1D9E75' : '#9DCFB7')),
        borderWidth: 0,
        borderRadius: 3,
      }]
    },
    options: {
      indexAxis: 'y',
      responsive: true,
      maintainAspectRatio: false,
      layout: { padding: { right: 28 } },
      plugins: {
        legend: { display: false },
        tooltip: { callbacks: { label: (ctx) => {
          const d = stateData[ctx.dataIndex];
          return `${d.v > 0 ? '+' : ''}${d.v}% premium · ${d.n.toLocaleString()} total postings`;
        }}}
      },
      scales: {
        x: {
          grid: { color: 'rgba(128,128,128,0.12)' },
          ticks: { color: '#64748b', callback: (v) => (v > 0 ? '+' : '') + v + '%' },
          title: { display: true, text: 'AI vs non-AI median salary gap', color: '#64748b' }
        },
        y: { grid: { display: false }, ticks: { color: '#334155', font: { size: 12 } } }
      },
      animation: { duration: 700 }
    }
  });
}

// Initialize when DOM is ready
if (document.readyState === 'loading') {
  document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', initCharts);
} else {
  initCharts();
}
</script>
```
